title: Layered soy candle -- bergamot and rosemary
subtitle: A two-layer soy wax candle with distinct citrus and herbal scent zones
excerpt: Two pours separated by a partial set create visible layers in the jar. The lower layer uses bergamot for a bright citrus base; the upper layer adds rosemary for a fresher, herbal finish. As the candle burns down, the scent shifts between the two zones.
sourceNotes: Layered candle technique: standard candle-craft reference. Essential oil usage in soy wax at reduced concentration for top-note oils: candle formulation practice.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. The two-pour technique takes about 3 hours total.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure for 48 hours for best fragrance throw.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Keep two years at room temperature away from direct sunlight. Discard if the surface turns oily or the scent has faded.

glossaryTerms.definition: Cold-pressed or steam-distilled bergamot peel oil. A bright citrus-floral top note with an Earl Grey quality. Use FCF (bergapten-free) versions for any product applied to skin.

content.text: Layered candles require patience: the first pour must be set but not fully cured before the second pour goes on, or the layers will merge. Aim for a surface that has set to a matte finish but is not yet hard to the touch -- about 60 to 90 minutes at room temperature.
is a citrus top note used at low concentration (3%) to prevent it burning off too quickly during use. Rosemary is a mid note that performs reasonably well in soy wax.

items.prepNote: for bottom layer (about 3% of 70 g wax); about 40 drops
for top layer (about 4% of 70 g wax); about 60 drops
CD-12 for a 6 to 7 cm jar

content.text: Stick the wick tab to the jar base. Melt 70 g of soy wax to 70°C. Cool to 57°C. Add 2 g of bergamot essential oil and stir. Pour into the jar to halfway. Leave at room temperature for 60 to 90 minutes until the surface is matte and semi-firm -- it should dent slightly under fingertip pressure but not be liquid.

Melt the remaining 70 g of soy wax. Cool to 55°C -- a slightly lower pour temperature prevents the heat from melting the layer beneath. Add 3 g of rosemary essential oil and stir. Pour gently over the back of a spoon held just above the first layer surface -- this cushions the flow and prevents the two layers from merging. Fill to 1.5 cm from the rim. Leave for two hours to set fully.
Trim the wick to 5 mm. Cure for 48 hours. The boundary between the two layers will be visible as a subtle difference in the white wax tone.

The bergamot bottom layer burns first; the rosemary top layer comes last. Allow the first burn to melt all the way to the jar edges before extinguishing. Trim the wick to 5 mm before every subsequent burn.
